title: Inflexibility, renormalized volume, and Weil-Petersson geometry
speaker: Jeff Brock

proof_stages.label: Coarse volume–Weil-Petersson comparison via pants distance
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up the problem of comparing hyperbolic three-manifold volume with Weil-Petersson translation length and recalls the earlier coarse theorem obtained by factoring both sides through pants-complex distance.

proof_stages.label: Quasi-Fuchsian double-iteration reduction
proof_stages.summary: A pseudo-Anosov mapping class is studied through quasi-Fuchsian manifolds with conformal boundaries iterated in opposite directions, whose limit is the cyclic cover of the fibered mapping torus.

proof_stages.label: Inflexibility asymptotics for iterated convex cores
proof_stages.summary: Geometric inflexibility supplies the analytic control needed to compare the convex-core volume of the double-iterate quasi-Fuchsian manifolds with the corresponding number of mapping-torus fundamental domains.

proof_stages.label: Renormalized volume and its explicit variational bound
proof_stages.summary: Renormalized volume is introduced as a canonical replacement for convex-core volume, and its first-variation formula together with Nahari's bound and Cauchy-Schwarz yields an explicit upper bound in terms of Weil-Petersson distance.

proof_stages.label: Fibered mapping-torus inequality from the quasi-Fuchsian estimate
proof_stages.summary: Applying the quasi-Fuchsian renormalized-volume estimate to double iterates, dividing by the iterate length, and using the inflexibility asymptotic gives the explicit bound of mapping-torus volume by Weil-Petersson translation length, with a weaker Teichmuller corollary and extensions to punctured surfaces.

proof_stages.label: Effective systole and diameter consequences
proof_stages.summary: The explicit volume-to-length inequality is combined with known minimal-volume hyperbolic three-manifolds and degeneration of Weil-Petersson axes to obtain concrete lower bounds for Weil-Petersson systoles, inradii, and diameters of moduli spaces.

examples.name: Low-complexity pants moves and the Farey graph
examples.note: The one-holed torus and four-holed sphere pictures make the elementary moves in the pants complex concrete, with the Farey graph illustrating the resulting distance in the simplest case.
examples.engagement_reason: He concretely unpacks pants moves on the one-holed torus and four-holed sphere, relates them to Farey-graph edges via intersections/determinants, and explains distance as edge-counting.

passes.reason: once-punctured-torus/four-holed-sphere pants moves are the minimal nontrivial pants-complex cases, modeled by the highly regular Farey graph

examples.name: Large Dehn twists as a metric warning
examples.note: The speaker uses high Dehn twisting to explain why pants distance and hyperbolic volume can remain coarsely bounded while Teichmuller length grows, motivating the sharper Weil-Petersson focus.
examples.engagement_reason: He analyzes explicit high-twist mapping classes, describing their Dehn-twist factors, bounded volumes, limiting axes, and limiting translation length/imaginary-axis behavior.

passes.reason: large Dehn-twist iterations are used to show Teichmuller translation length can grow while volume stays bounded, warning against a naive metric comparison
large Dehn-twist families are reducible/cusp-direction examples used to show Teichmüller length can grow while volume stays bounded
High powers of Dehn twists are reducible/parabolic mapping classes used to warn that Teichmuller distance can grow while volume or pants distance stays bounded.
Powers of a Dehn twist are reducible/parabolic mapping classes fixing a curve and are used to show Teichmuller distance can grow while the associated volumes stay bounded.

Large Dehn-twist directions are reducible/parabolic boundary-type cases whose limiting behavior makes Teichmüller length grow while volume stays bounded, so they serve as a warning against a Teichmüller-volume comparison.
Large Dehn-twist families are used as a warning that Teichmüller translation length can grow while volume stays bounded, obstructing a tight Teichmüller-volume comparison.
Large Dehn-twist directions are used to warn that Teichmüller length can grow while volume stays bounded, and twists are reducible/cuspidal degenerations rather than typical pseudo-Anosov behavior.

Large Dehn-twist powers are reducible annular/limiting mapping-class behavior and are used to show a naive Teichmuller-metric volume comparison can fail because twist distance grows while volume stays bounded.
used to show Teichmüller distance can grow while volume stays bounded, via a large-twist cusp/Dehn-filling limiting regime
Large Dehn-twist powers form a cusp/limiting reducible-twist family used to show Teichmuller-type metric comparison can fail or be non-tight.
large Dehn-twist/Dehn-filling sequences degenerate toward a cusped limit and are used to show Teichmüller length need not track bounded volume
large Dehn-twist families are used as a limiting warning where Teichmuller length grows while volume stays bounded, obstructing a naive metric comparison

examples.name: Schläfli-formula analogy
examples.note: The classical hyperbolic polyhedron variational formula is invoked only as intuition for viewing the renormalized-volume variation as a continuous analogue.
examples.engagement_reason: He invokes the Schläfli formula only as an intuition/analogy for the variational formula, without deriving or applying it to a concrete case.
